coredata.dc:title: Preparation of coal-based C-Dots/TiO2 and its visible-light photocatalytic characteristics for degradation of pulping black liquor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Bo
Maimaiti, Halidan
Zhang, De-Dong
Xu, Bo
Wei, Ming
coredata.dc:description: Coal-based carbon-dots (C-Dots) with a graphene-like structure is prepared from the coal mined of Xinjiang Wucaiwan by an acid/ultrasonic mixed oxidation technique. Subsequently, the C-Dots is combined with the in-situ synthesized TiO2 particles, yielding C-Dots/TiO2 nanoparticles. These were characterized by TEM, XRD, TG, Raman, BET, XPS, EDS, UV–vis DRS and PL, respectively. It was found that the C-Dots/TiO2 are subsphaeroidal particles formed by the deposition of C-Dots on the surface of TiO2. Those particles are linked to various functional groups such as CC, COH/COC, CO, TiO, and are 30–50nm in diameter and evenly distributed without agglomeration. After combination with C-Dots, the band gap of C-Dots/TiO2 decreases from 3.2eV in the case of pure TiO2 to 2.9eV, the PL intensity of C-Dots/TiO2 centered at 400nm dropped significantly, indicates stronger visible light photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic activities of the nanocomposites are evaluated by the photocatalytic degradation of cotton pulp cooking black liquor in the presence of visible light irradiation. The composite catalysts exhibited higher photocatalytic activity for the degradation of organic pollutants than pure TiO2 and coal-based C-dots, which is due to the synergistic effect of C-Dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coal
C-Dots
TiO2
Photocatalysis
Pulping black liquor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental testing on visible-light photocatalytic degradation of pulping black liquor
Characteristics of C-Dots/TiO2 for visible-light photocatalytic degradation of pulping black liquor
Mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of black liquor by C-Dots/TiO2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of (a) coal-based C-dots, (b) TiO2 and (c) C-Dots/TiO2.

Physical characterizations of (a) C-Dots, (b)TiO2 and (c) C-Dots/TiO2. (A) XRD pattern, (B) TGA curve of C-Dots/TiO2, (C) Raman spectra and (D) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm of TiO2 and C-Dots/TiO2.
XPS spectrum of coal-based C-dots and C-Dots/TiO2.

Photocatalytic activity of C-Dots/TiO2. A. Treating result of pulping black liquid (a)under the dark condition in the presence of C-Dots/TiO2 and by photocatalytic degradation in the presence of (b) no catalyst, (c) C-Dots/TiO2, (d) coal-based C-dots, (e) pure TiO2; B. Results of CODcr removal rate of pulping black liquid and catalyst residual quality using the C-Dots/TiO2 at optimized conditions versus cycle number.
Total ion chromatogram (TIC) of pulping black liquor before (B) and after (A) photocatalytic degradation in the presence of the C-Dots/TiO2.
(A) DR UV–vis spectra; (B)Curves of energy band gap (Eg) value; (C) FL spectra of (a)TiO2 and (b) C-Dots/TiO2..

head.ce:title: Preparation of coal-based C-Dots/TiO2 and its visible-light photocatalytic characteristics for degradation of pulping black liquor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry and Chemical Industry, Xinjiang University, Urumqi 830046, Xinjiang, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemistry and Chemical Industry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Coal-based carbon-dots (C-Dots) with a graphene-like structure is prepared from the coal mined of Xinjiang Wucaiwan by an acid/ultrasonic mixed oxidation technique. Subsequently, the C-Dots is combined with the in-situ synthesized TiO2 particles, yielding C-Dots/TiO2 nanoparticles. These were characterized by TEM, XRD, TG, Raman, BET, XPS, EDS, UV–vis DRS and PL, respectively. It was found that the C-Dots/TiO2 are subsphaeroidal particles formed by the deposition of C-Dots on the surface of TiO2. Those particles are linked to various functional groups such as CC, COH/COC, CO, TiO, and are 30–50nm in diameter and evenly distributed without agglomeration. After combination with C-Dots, the band gap of C-Dots/TiO2 decreases from 3.2eV in the case of pure TiO2 to 2.9eV, the PL intensity of C-Dots/TiO2 centered at 400nm dropped significantly, indicates stronger visible light photocatalytic activity. The photocatalytic activities of the nanocomposites are evaluated by the photocatalytic degradation of cotton pulp cooking black liquor in the presence of visible light irradiation. The composite catalysts exhibited higher photocatalytic activity for the degradation of organic pollutants than pure TiO2 and coal-based C-dots, which is due to the synergistic effect of C-Dots.

ce:section.ce:para: The pulping black liquor refers to the wastewater generated from cellulose as a result of high temperature and strong alkaline during the process of preparing papermaking wood pulp or chemical fiber cotton pulp. It consists of three parts, namely alkaline cooking wastewater (cooking black liquor), bleaching wastewater and water-driven wastewater. Among these three parts, the cooking black liquor contains a large amount of cotton linter, tannin, pectin, fat, wax, wood sugar, solid, free alkali, etc., whose key characteristics include intense odor, dark color, high chemical oxygen demand (COD), strong alkalinity and poor bioavailability [1]. It is therefore a key pollution source in the pulping industry. As a result, developing an effective method to treat the cooking black liquor is of paramount significance to the environmental protection concerning the pulp industry. Currently, there are three methods for treating pulping black liquor, which are based on physicochemistry, biology and chemistry, respectively. Specifically, the relevant techniques include coagulation, adsorption, membrane separation, biochemical techniques (e.g., anaerobic and aerobic bio-chemical treatment), electrochemistry and advanced oxidation techniques (e.g., ozone oxidation, Fenton oxidation) etc. [2]. Despite that these techniques are close to becoming mature, the intrinsic limitations associated with these techniques render the treatment outcome suboptimal, and in some cases result in high cost and limited economic viability. Therefore, the development of a low-cost and high-efficiency pulping black liquor treatment technique carries a major practical significance.

The photocatalytic oxidation based degradation method is a novel environmental protection technique, which with the presence of light, decomposes the organic contaminants into small molecules (e.g., CO2 and H2O) via photocatalysis. Its promising applications concerning wastewater treatment, air purification, utilization of solar energy, and anti-microbial and self-cleaning functionality have attracted wide attention within the research community [3–5]. The practically used photocatalysts include conventional metallic quantum dots (e.g., cadmium sulfide, cadmium selenide, etc.), nano-sized semiconductor (e.g., titanium dioxide, cuprous oxide, etc.) and Fenton reagents (Fe2+/H202), etc. [5–7]. The advantages of this technique include efficient utilization of photoenergy, minimal secondary pollution and efficient oxidative degradation.On the other hand, the disadvantages of this technique are linked to the deficiencies of the used photocatalysts. Typical examples include the potential heavy metal pollution caused by the metallic quantum dots (e.g., cadmium sulfide), the limited amount of ultraviolet radiation in the sunlight that can be absorbed by TiO2-based catalyst, and the low quantum efficiency, etc. [8]. Moreover, the Fenton reagents may lead to massive precipitation of Fe(OH)2 or Fe(OH)3 in the system [9], which may increase the operational difficulty. Therefore, the development of an environmentally friendly photocatalyst which generates zero secondary pollution, functions within a wide range of excitation wavelengths, and possesses high quantum efficiency carries major academic value.

Carbon nanomaterials have become the focus of scientific researchers at all levels in the world [10–16] because of its own potential advantages and their broad application in the fields of chemistry, physics and materials science [6,17–19]. Such as fluorescent Carbon Dots (C-Dots) of them, it resembles traditional quantum dots in terms of fluorescent characteristics (e.g., capable of emitting stable and strong fluorescent light, possessing tunable excitation and emission wavelength, up-converting functionality and good visible fluorescent light emission capability, etc.), also, it is an effective electron donor and acceptor. Upon light excitation, it can generate photoelectrons and holes, and in the meantime can leverage its intrinsic surface defects to suppress the recombination of photoelectrons and holes [20–22]. Due to this attribute, it can be used as a type of standalone photocatalyst, or can be doped with other elements or used jointly with other materials so as to generate composite photocatalysts with improved catalytic efficiency [23–25]. Based on the presently published papers, there are few studies on the preparation of fluorescent C-Dots using coal as raw material, and most research work concerning the C-Dots photocatalytic activity is focused on applying photocatalytic degradation to certain pure organic dyes and toxic gas, or the field of catalytic hydrogenation. The research concerning the photocatalytic degradation of the complex pulping black liquor has been seldom reported.

To address this shortcoming, the present study adopts mixed acid/ultrasound hybrid method to treat the HNO3-pretreated coal mined from Xinjiang Wucaiwan. The multi-stage washing method is used to isolate the polymerized aromatics structural materials from coal, which is in turn linked to the oxygen containing functional groups (e.g., nitro group, etc.), so as to produce coal-based C-Dots assuming a multi-layer graphene structure based on sp2-bonded carbon atoms. The resulting product is in turn combined with the in-situ synthesized TiO2 particles, giving rise to C-Dots/TiO2 nanoparticles. Besides examining its structure and optical characteristics, the present study also uses the cotton pulp cooking black liquor generated by acid precipitation as the treatment target, based on which the performance of using C-Dots/TiO2 as a photocatalytic degradation reagent for treating pulping black liquor is studied, results C-Dots/TiO2 is found to have a higher photocatalytic activity than a pure TiO2, which can be very useful for cleaning real industrial wastewater.

The coal sample used here is obtained from the Wucaiwan coal mine at China's Xinjiang Zundong coal field. The sample is grinded and filtered through a 200# mesh sieve, followed by a 4h desiccation at 105°C; The cotton pulp cooking black liquor is obtained from the Cooking Department at China's Xinjiang Manasi-Aoyang Technology Co., Ltd. The reagents, e.g., H2SO4, HNO3, CH3COOH, Ti(OBu)4, C2H5OH, etc., are all of Analytical Reagent (AR) grade, and are commercially available.
The disposable bag filters used here are produced by American Spectrum Labs (molecular weight cutoff 100–500), and are purchased from Shanghai Tongshan Biological Technology Co., Ltd.

2g of coal sample filtered through a 200# mesh sieve is weighted and added to 150mL of 2.6M nitric acid aqueous solution, which is stirred at room temperature for 24h, followed by reflux stirring for another 24h. The solution is subsequently cooled to room temperature, and is subject to centrifugal segregation so as to isolate out the coal cinder. The product is then desiccated. Also, 0.5g of sample is placed in a dissolving bottle, after which 25mL of mixed acid (H2SO4:HNO3 =3:1 by volume) is added to the bottle, which is subsequently subject to ultrasonic dispersion at 60°C for 12h. The resulting product is transferred to a centrifugal tube with 20mL of purified water added, and is then subject to a centrifugal separation at 10000rpm. After the separation, the supernatant liquid in the tube is removed, and 20mL of purified water is used to rinse the precipitant for twice. The centrifugal separation (10000rpm) is employed again to remove the water, and 30mL of purified water is added to the tube. The tube is then stirred for 1min to disperse the precipitate back the liquid phase. Subsequently, the centrifugal separation is conducted and the resulting supernatant liquid is collected, which is then placed in a dialysis bag and undergoes dialysis in water for 72h (throughout which water is changed incessantly) until the pH value reaches 7. This process generates an aqueous dispersion of coal-based C-Dots. After desiccation, 0.0618g of black brown coal-based C-Dots is finally obtained.

Certain amount of reactant based on C2H5OH: H2O: CH3COOH=7: 4: 0.5 (by molar ratio) is weighted, well mixed and labeled as liquid A. Also, certain amount of anhydrous ethanol and tetrabutyl titanate is mixed following C2H5OH: Ti(OBu)4 =7: 1(by molar ratio) and labeled as liquid B. Subsequently, liquid B is subject to a 10min of stirring, and then liquid A is gradually dripped into liquid B. The solution is let stand for 24h, resulting in a TiO2 gel. The produced gel is desiccated at 80°C for 6h, grinded, and placed in a Muffle furnace whose temperature is slowly ramped up to 450°C. The sample is burnt at this constant temperature for 2h, and then cooled to room temperature, giving rise to TiO2 particles.

The C-Dots/TiO2 is prepared by hydrothermal method: 25mg of coal-based C-dots and 20mL of ethanol is dissolved in 40mL of ultrapure water. The mixtures is ultrasonic dispersion at room temperature for 1h. Then, 250mg TiO2 obtained in the previous step is added, and the ultrasound is continued at room temperature for another 1h. After mixing evenly, they were transferred into a 100mL Teflon-lined stainless steel reactor and treated at 120°C for 5h. After cooling down to room temperature, the mixed liquor is subject to centrifugal segregation so as to isolate out the sediment. Then, they were taken out, rinsed with ultrapure water, and desiccated at 60°C for 12h, giving rise to the black brown coal-based C-Dots/TiO2.

The aqueous dispersion of C-Dots and ethanol dispersion of TiO2 and C-Dots/TiO2 are dripped on a copper mesh and dried out. The H-600 transmission electron microscope manufactured by Hitachi, Ltd. is employed to examine the morphological structure of various particles; the M18XHF22-SRAX-ray diffractometer manufactured by MAC is used to conduct XRD characterization on coal-based C-Dots, TiO2 and C-Dots/TiO2. The experimental condition is set as follows: Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.154056nm), and scanning range 2θ =10–70°; TG analysis of the C-Dots/TiO2 is determined by PE-DTA/1700 thermal analyzer in an air atmosphere with a heating rate of 10°C/min from room temperature to 1000°C; Raman spectroscopy are recorded using a BRUKER VERTEX 70 (532nm laser) system; Nitrogen physisorption was carried out at 77K with an ASAP 2050 tended Pressure Sorption Analyzer; the ESCALAB-250X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy system (XPS) manufactured by Thermo Fisher is adopted to analyze the surface morphology of coal-based C-Dots and C-Dots/TiO2; the UV–vis diffuse reflective spectrum (UV-vis DRS) technique is used to measure UV–vis DRS of C-Dots, TiO2 and C-Dots/TiO2, during which the BaSO4 is used for reference, and the testing wavelength ranges between 250nm and 750nm;the aqueous dispersion of TiO2 and C-Dots/TiO2 is transferred into cuvette after dilution and then measured by Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba, France) for emission spectra at specific excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental testing on visible-light photocatalytic degradation of pulping black liquor
ce:section.ce:para:
Certain amount of cotton pulp cooking black liquor pretreated with acid precipitation (CODcr =2545mg/L) and C-Dots/TiO2 is placed in a photochemical quartz reactor with an internal water circulation system. The reaction is carried out in darkness for 30min with magnetic stirring constantly applied. The sample is illuminated with a xenon lamp (with 400nm Long-wave pass filter added so as to ensure that the incident light is exclusively visible light) to trigger the photocatalytic degradation reaction. During the experimental process, 10mL of reactant liquid is drawn from the reactor for every 30min, which is then subject to the ultracentrifugal sedimentation (10,000r/min) so as to separate out the supernatant liquor. The Potassium bichromate titration methodis then used to measure the CODcr in the sample, and Eq. (1) is then employed to calculate the CODcr removal rate RTCODcr [26].
(1)
RT
COD Cr
=
C
0
− C
C
0
× 100 %
Where C0 and C denote the CODcr of black liquor before and after light treatment, respectively.

Certain amount of water sample is weighted and used with dichloromethaneto perform alternate extraction under acidic, neutral and alkaline conditions. The extraction fluids are combined, followed by using anhydrous sodium sulfate to remove the water in the solution and concentrate the extraction liquid to 2mL. The resulting liquid is filtered and a gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GCMS-QP2010 Ultra) is employed to conduct qualitative analysis on the obtained sample.

Chromatography condition: Chromatography columns are DB-5 capillary columns (30m×0.25mm×0.25m); carrier gas(He):1mL/min; fractional flow ratio:1:10; inlet temperature:280°C; sample volume: 1μL; temperature program: 40°C (maintained for 3min)→200°C (5°C/min, maintained for 3min)→300°C (10°C/min, maintained for 4min), solvent delay: 4min.

Mass spectrometry condition: Electron Impact Source (EI); Impact Voltage: 70eV; Electron Multiplier Voltage: 1.2kV; Mass Scanning Range 33–500amu; Mass Spectrum Library: NIST14.L; Solvent delay: 4min.

Coal is a type of naturally occurring macromolecule. Its macroscopic structure contains the amorphous carbon regions formed by aliphatic compounds and the nanoscale crystal carbon regions formed by polymerized aromatics [27]. Among these two regions, the crystal carbon regions are abundant, within which each crystal carbon is of similar size with that of quantum dots. A variety of methods can be used to peel the crystal carbon off so as to make carbon dots [28,29]. The TEM testing results of the produced C-Dots indicate that, as shown in Fig. 1 (a), the coal-based C-Dots prepared based on mixed acid oxidation method by peeling from the dust-free Wucaiwan coal are spherical particles whose diameter is less than 10nm. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy images show that the nanoparticles have lattice fringes similar to the structure of graphite (illustrations of Fig. 1(a)). The lattice interval is 0.21nm, which is close to the (100) crystal face of graphite [30].The experimentally prepared pure TiO2 are grain-like particles (Fig. 1(b)) whose size ranges between 20 and 30nm, with agglomeration observed. In contrast, the C-Dots/TiO2 emerges as well-dispersed nanoparticles with slightly larger size ranging from 30 to 50nm (Fig. 1(c)). Obviously, as can be seen, the composite particles are formed by the deposition of C-Dots on the surface of TiO2, and the carbon coating can prevent the TiO2 nanoparticles from aggregating.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern can illustrate the atom distribution inside the crystal. Fig. 2 A shows the XRD pattern of coal-based C-Dots (a), TiO2(b), and the composite of C-Dots/TiO2(c). It can be seen that the characteristic diffraction peaks of pure TiO2 at 2θ =25.2°, 37.7°, 47.8°, 53.6°, 54.8° and 62.5° correspond to the crystal faces of (101), (004), (200), (105), (211) and (204), respectively, associated with the anatase form of TiO2 (JCPDS:21-1272), showing that the TiO2 in the C-Dots/TiO2 is anatase-type TiO2.

The coal-based C-Dots have wide diffraction peaks corresponding to the (002) crystal face of graphite within the range of 15–40°. However, the peaks corresponding to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204) crystal faces of the anatase TiO2 in C-Dots/TiO2 obtained by hydrothermal reaction all become lower and wider, which is likely due to the fact that the C-Dots assuming graphite structure deposits on the TiO2 surface, decrease the crystallinity of TiO2 in the composite particles. Likewise, the presence of TiO2 can also affect the diffraction peaks of C-Dots, no detected the characteristic peaks of C-Dots in the C-Dots/TiO2 due to the high dispersion and low crystallinity of doped C-Dots. Fig. 2B presents the thermogravimetry analyses (TGA) curves of C-Dots/TiO2, it can be concluded that the content of C-Dots in the C-Dots/TiO2 is evaluated to be ∼8.84wt%.

Raman spectra are recorded to investigate the surface composition and structures of C-Dots, TiO2 and C-Dots/TiO2 (Fig. 2C). The vibrational peaks at 146, 396, 515 and 636cm−1 are observed in the Raman spectra of both TiO2 and C-Dots/TiO2, well consistent with the results reported in literature [31]. In addition, compared with TiO2, there are two extra characteristic peaks located at 1352 and 1584cm−1 in the Raman spectra of C-Dots/TiO2, corresponding to disorder carbon (D-band) and graphite carbon (G-band) attributed by the C-Dots coated on the surface of TiO2.

The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurement is carried out to investigate the surface and porous structures of C-Dots/TiO2. As shown in Fig. 2D, N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of C-Dots/TiO2 and TiO2 can be identified as type IV isotherm (IUPAC), suggesting the mesoporous structure. According to the BET analysis, the specifc surface areas of the TiO2 and C-Dots/TiO2 are measured to be 7.9 and 46.3m2 g−1, respectively. The large surface area and porous structure helps to increase the concentration of organic pollutants on the surface of photocatalyst, facilitating the contact of the reactive species with the organic molecules in black liquor.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is used to analyze the surface morphology of coal-based C-Dots and C-Dots/TiO2, which reveals that the prepared coal-based C-Dots exhibit three characteristic peaks at 285.0, 401.5 and 530.9eV, (Fig. 3 A), corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. This indicates that the coal-based C-Dots mainly consist of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements. The mass fractions of three elements are 72.52%, 2.06% and 23.79%, respectively. This shows that the prepared coal-based C-Dots resemble other types of carbon nanoparticles in terms of the carbon-based chain linked to copious oxygen atoms. Meanwhile, due to the fact that the raw coal contains certain amount of nitrogen and the preparation of coal-based C-Dots involves nitric acid as an oxidizing reagent, the product contains small amount of N element. Fig. 3B illustrates the high-resolution XPS spectrum diagram of the coal-based C-Dots C1s. It can be seen that there are three separate peaks at 284.8, 286.7 and 288.8eV, corresponding to CC/COH/COC, CN and CO bonds [32], respectively. The spectrum of O1s is split into two peaks coming from the binding energy of CO/COH and COC (Fig. 3C). It shows that certain oxygen containing functional groups, e.g., carboxyl, hydroxyl, epoxy and nitro group, exist on the C-Dots surface.

Fig. 3D–F are the XPS full spectrum diagrams of C-Dots/TiO2, and the peak analytical spectrum diagrams of C1s, O1s and Ti2p, respectively. Fig. 3D shows peaks of C1s, N1s, O1s and Ti2p, indicating that the surface of composite particles has C, N, O and Ti elements. The mass fractions of these four elements are 25.41%, 0.91%, 56.09% and 17.58%, respectively. Compared to that of C-Dots, the fraction of oxygen element in C-Dots/TiO2 is increased significantly, which is attributed to the presence of TiO2. Fig. 3E shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum diagram of C1s in C-Dots/TiO2, which exhibits peaks associated with CC, COH/COC and CO bonds at 284.8, 285.9 and 288.9 eV[32], respectively. This pattern basically agrees well with Fig. 3B, indicating that the carbon in C-Dots/TiO2 is derived from coal-based C-Dots. The reason for the disappearance of CN in Fig. 3E is that after the addition of Ti and O, the relative content of N is decreased. The high-resolution XPS spectrum diagram of O1s (Fig. 3F) at 530.5eV exhibits a peak associated with the TiO functional group in TiO2 [33]. Also, it exhibits peaks associated with the functional groups CO and CO at 531.0eV and 532.8eV on the C-Dots surface [34], respectively. The two observations jointly indicate that the composite particles consist of C-Dots and TiO2. Illustrations of Fig. 3F illustrates the high-resolution XPS spectrum diagram of Ti2p, which exhibits two peaks of varying sizes at 458.7eV and 464.5eV, corresponding to TiO2 electron binding energies Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2, respectively. This observation agrees well with the XRD testing result, showing that the TiO2 in the C-Dots/TiO2 composite particles is anatase-typeTiO2 with high purity [35].The EDS testing result of C-Dots/TiO2 further proves that C-Dots/TiO2 consists of C, N, O and Ti (Fig. S1 and Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of C-Dots/TiO2 for visible-light photocatalytic degradation of pulping black liquor
ce:section.ce:para: The Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD) of organic wastewater is a key indicator of the pollutant content in the wastewater. The COD removal rate of samples during the photocatalytic degradation process reflects the degree of photocatalytic degradation of organic matters in the system. Therefore, in the present experiment, we use the CODcr removal rate in pulping black liquor to measure the C-Dots/TiO2 reaction efficiency concerning visible-light photocatalytic degradation of pulping black liquor, which is in turn compared with the catalytic activity of the coal-based C-dots and pure TiO2, with the results shown in Fig. 4 A.

As shown in Fig. 4A, by applying C-Dots/TiO2 as the photocatalyst and conducting reaction in a dark room, the CODcr removal rate can reach as high as 20.5% (with 30min of stirring), with the CODcr removal rate in black liquor decreasing with reaction time (curve a). This is attributed to the fact that a dark environment inhibits the photocatalytic degradation mechanism, and the removal of CODcr in black liquor has to be solely based on the adsorption effect of C-Dots; by providing 3h of direct illumination without applying catalyst, the CODcr removal rate in black liquor is merely 25.48%, indicating the low efficiency of photo-oxidation reaction without catalyst (curve b).By applying C-Dots/TiO2 as the catalyst under illumination, after 3h of reaction, the CODcr removal rate in black liquor has reached 81.2% (curve c), which far exceeds the CODcr removal rate when the coal-based C-dots and pure TiO2 are used the photocatalyst, respectively. When applying the C-Dots as the photocatalyst, the removal rate of CODcr in black liquor is the highest at 90min, which is 58.9% (curve d), however, with the increase of irradiation time, the removal rate of CODcr in black liquor decreased due to the C-Dots over adsorption of organic compounds in black liquor and leads to a decrease in the ability of generate photoelectrons and holes; when pure TiO2 was used as photocatalyst, the highest CODcr removal rate in black liquor is 32.8% (curve e), which was obviously due to the poor utilization of visible light by TiO2.

Considering the practical application of catalyst, we recycle the C-Dots/TiO2 after washing and drying, and investigated the performance of the catalyst for removing the CODcr in black liquor, and the mass loss of C-Dots/TiO2 in the process, the results are shown in Fig. 4B. The results showed that with the increase of the number of cycles, there was no significant change in the amount of photocatalyst recovered, but the removal rate of CODcr in black liquid is on the decline. When the number of cycles using C-Dots/TiO2 is 5, The removal rate of C-Dots/TiO2 to the CODcr in black liquid is reduced to 66.0%. Based on the above analysis, we believe that the reasons for the low cycle of C-Dots/TiO2 is that the composite particle is contaminated by certain organic matter in black liquor.

Compared with other nano catalysts reported in the literature [36–39], in spite of the rate of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants is slightly lower, but the pollutants used in this experiment is not a single dye, which contains a variety of complex organic matter, is a real industrial wastewater. Therefore, we believe that the C-Dots/TiO2 photocatalyst prepared in this paper has certain usability.

Following the aforementioned experimental methodology, GC–MS tests are carried out concerning the samples collected before and after the photocatalytic treatment of coal-based C-Dots/TiO2 photocatalytic. Based on online automatic search, a variety of measured chromatographic peaks are qualitatively analyzed so as to determine the type and relative concentration of compounds in the water samples, with results shown in Fig. 5 and Table S2.

Fig. 5 shows that the cotton pulp black liquor subject to acid precipitation treatment has 20 peaks (only those exceeding 1.5% are counted here). Those samples subject to C-Dots/TiO2 photocatalytic degradation treatment only have 8 peaks. The types of organic compounds (counted based on the number of chromatographic peaks) are reduced by 60%. The concentration of the organic matters (measured by the area of chromatographic peak) is greatly reduced. Specifically, the total area of chromatographic peak associated with acid precipitation treatment of cotton pulp black liquor is 103296749, while the total chromatographic peak area of water samples post the C-Dots/TiO2 photocatalytic degradation is 20293768. The total removal rate reaches 80.35%, which agrees well with the aforementioned CODCr removal results.

Table S2 shows that the relatively simple organic compounds in cotton pulp black liquor, e.g., lipids, acids, ketones, alcohols, paraffins, and phenols, etc., can easily be treated with visible-light photocatalytic degradation, or converted into other organisms with the presence of C-Dots/TiO2; those stable compounds with complex structures, e.g., Toluene, Cyclopentanol,2-chloro-,trans-, Apocynin, Estra-1,3,5(10)-trien-17.beta.-ol, Phenol,4,4′-(1-methylethylidene)bis-, etc., can be slightly degraded by C-Dots/TiO2 photocatalysts; and the heterocyclic compounds with nitrogen atoms in their structures, e.g., 3-alkyl-2-methoxypyrazines, 5,10-Diethoxy-2,3,7,8-tetrahydro-1H,6H-Dipyrrolo[1,2-a:1′,2′-d]pyrazine and Pyrrolo[1,2-a]pyrazine-1,4-dione,hexahydro-3-(2-methylpropyl)-, can barely be degraded by C-Dots/TiO2 photocatalytic degradation, specifically, the concentration of this kind of organic compounds in the last samples treated by photocatalytic degradation reaches 76.723%, making it possible to use the organic compounds obtained through separation and purification as a product with high additional value. The content of these heterocyclic compounds is lower in front of the sample before C-Dots/TiO2 photocatalytic degradation treatment, speculation is likely to be the compounds containing hydroxyl in the black liquor first into diols, and then condensation cyclization reaction with diamine generates the heterocyclic compounds with nitrogen atoms in their structures. This needs to be confirmed by studying the changes of the organic matter in black liquor in different time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of black liquor by C-Dots/TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation technology for pulping black liquor is developed based on photochemical oxidation technology, which is essentially based on the photon-induced oxidation-reduction reaction, includes the following basic steps [40]: (1) under illumination, the electrons on the valence band of catalytic materials are stimulated to jump to the conduction band, giving rise to photoelectron-hole pairs(e−-h+); (2) the photoelectron-hole pairs detach from the bulk material and migrate to the surface of the photocatalytic material. Meanwhile, a portion of photoelectron-hole pairs may recombine in the core or at the surface; (3) the photoelectrons (e−) and holes (h+) having migrated to the surface of catalytic material participate in the oxidation-reduction reaction with the organic compounds absorbed on the surface of catalytic material. Specifically, the hole h+ has a strong ability to acquire electron (i.e., a strong oxidizer), which can attract the electrons in the organic molecules absorbed to the surface of the catalyst in the black liquor, so as to directly oxidize those organic molecules. Conversely, the photoelectron e− has strong reduction ability, which can react with the O2 molecules absorbed to the surface of catalyst and generates superoxide free radical (O2−) with supreme oxidation ability, enhances the oxidative degradation of organic molecules. It therefore can be inferred that the photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst is related to the factors such as the utilization of the catalyst to the light, the ability of the catalyst giving rise to photoelectron-hole pairs and to separate them. Therefore, by measuring the UV–vis DRS, band gap curves and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of TiO2 and C-Dots/TiO2, we have verified the ability of the prepared C-Dots/TiO2 for promoting the generation of photoelectron-hole and separate them with stronger catalytic activity under the illumination of visible light.

It can be seen from Fig. 6 A that TiO2 only absorbs light in ultraviolet region, while the composite product C-Dots/TiO2 derived from the combination of TiO2 and C-Dots is influenced by C-Dots, can absorb light in both ultraviolet and visible regions, whose absorption band is red shifted and absorption strength also significantly exceeds that of TiO2.

The UV–vis DRS is used to measure the correlation between Kubelka-Munk function F(R∞) (R∞ denotes total reflection coefficient) and the incident light wavelength λ of TiO2 and composite nanoparticles C-Dots/TiO2. Subsequently, Eq. (2) is employed to obtain the curve of [F(R∞)·hv]2-hv (Fig. 6B).
(2)
F
(
R
∞
)
=
B
(
hv −
E
g
)
1
2
hv
Where hv denotes photon energy, Eg denotes band gap, and B is a constant.

By extrapolating the linear part of the curve to F(R∞)=0, one can obtain the light absorption threshold Eg of the semiconductor TiO2(a) and the band gap curves of composite nanoparticles of C-Dots/TiO2(b) from the intercept [41], as shown in Fig. 6(B). TiO2 is a good electron donor, its band gap is 3.2 eV(which agrees well with literature report[42]), after TiO2 compound with the electron acceptor C-Dots, there may be a synergistic effect between them, the band gap of C-Dots/TiO2 is decreases to 2.9eV, indicating that the response of composite particles to visible light is enhanced, which can generate photo-electrons and holes with enhanced catalytic activity under the illumination of visible light [43].

The photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst is not only related to the intensity and wavelength of excitation, but also related to the recombination of photo-generated charges[44]. The PL emission measurement can be used to characterize the fate of electron-hole pairs in photocatalyst. The lower the PL emission intensity is, the lower the recombination efficiency of the photogenerated carriers is [45]. As evident in Fig. 6C, the TiO2 sample exhibited strong photoluminescence centered at 400nm originated from the C-Dots in the composite. In comparison, the intensity of this emission band dropped significantly for C-Dots/TiO2. This indicated the composite particle of C-Dots and TiO2 could inhibit the recombination of the photo-generate electron-hole pairs of TiO2 and improved their separation efficiency, leading to an enhancement of the photocatalytic performance. Besides, after TiO2 compound with the coal-based C-dots, the specific surface area of C-Dots/TiO2 increased greatly, this will help the catalyst to adsorb more organic matter in black liquor, which can improve the capability of C-Dots/TiO2 for photocatalytic degradation of black liquor.

The present study uses coal samples collected from Xinjiang Wucaiwan as the raw material, and produces coal-based carbon dots(C-Dots) based on mixed acid/ultrasound hybrid method. The produced C-Dots are smaller than 10nm. By combining the produced C-Dots with the in-situ synthesized TiO2 particles, C-Dots/TiO2 nanoparticles are produced.

The TEM, XRD, TG, Raman, BET, XPS and EDS testing results show that the prepared C-Dots/TiO2 are subsphaeroidal particles formed by the deposition of C-Dots on the surface of TiO2. Those particles are linked to various functional groups such as CC, COH/COC, CO, TiO, and are 30–50nm in diameter and evenly distributed without agglomeration.

By employing C-Dots/TiO2 as a visible-light photocatalyst for the photocatalytic degradation of pulping black liquor, after 3h treatment, a majority of organic compounds in black liquor (except for heterocyclic compounds containing nitrogen atoms) are degraded, leading to a CODcr removal rate of 81.2%, which is significantly superior to the visible-light photocatalytic effect of pure TiO2 and coal-based C-dots.

The curve analysis of UV–vis DRS, band gap and PL spectra indicates that after combination with C-Dots, the band gap of C-Dots/TiO2 decreases from 3.2eV in the case of pure TiO2 to 2.9eV, and compared with TiO2, the PL intensity of C-Dots/TiO2 centered at 400nm dropped significantly, indicating that the photocatalytic activity of visible light gets strengthened.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support of the State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals of Dalian University of Technology (KF1414) and the National Science Foundation of China (21663028).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2017.05.031.
